entry.title: Key User Adaptation: Powerful New Features & Seamless Transition to Developer Extensibility
entry.content:
With key user adaptation, you can change the user interface of SAP Fiori apps directly in the Fiori launchpad — intuitively and without having to write new code. The changes are upgrade-safe adaptations to SAP Fiori apps. Key users can open the UI Adaptation mode for a Fiori app directly from their user menu in the Fiori launchpad (menu entry Adapt UI).

A key user is a business expert who is authorized to make changes on behalf of other users — for example a team member who is interested in technology. The goal of key user extensibility is to empower business users to be able to adapt their working environment to suit their needs, without having to rely on system administrators.

 

What can key users change with key user adaptation?

Key users can perform the following changes in Fiori apps based on SAP Fiori elements:

  • Hide/show the information that shall be visible in the object page header
  • Hide/show tabs, sections, tables, or action buttons on the object page
  • Define the fields visible in forms on the object page
  • Set the configuration for tables (select all, selection mode, thresholds, view settings, export, etc.)
  • Set the configuration for the filter bar (clear button, usage of the semantic date range control)
  • Set the configuration for the complete list report / object page (variant management, initial load)
  • Change Text Arrangement (ID only, Text first, Text last, Text only)
  • Change Labels (global label changes)
  • Rename (only for a dedicated field – applies only locally to a form field)
  • Define the fields visible in the mass edit dialog. In the key user adaptation mode, all fields except static read-only fields are available.
  • And more

The scope was continuously enhanced. The bold items above are extremely powerful new features available since S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2508 / S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025 / On-Premise 2025. They are described in detail in the following sections.

 

New feature: configuration for tables

The Configuration functionality is available in key user adaptation mode when the context menu entry Configuration is selected on a table, on the filter bar, or on page level. The context menu on a table contains — for example in Fiori elements V4 — the following entries:

 

Among other things, the following settings can be configured here:

  • Enable Table Export — configures whether the Export button is displayed in the table.
  • Frozen Column Count — configures the count of columns that are always visible when scrolling horizontally.
  • Personalization — configures whether the table personalization settings are enabled. To configure the listed settings individually, choose Own Settings.
  • Row Count — configures the number of rows to be displayed in the table.
  • Width Including Column Header — configures whether the column header label is considered when calculating the column width.
  • Enable Select All — configures whether the Select All option is displayed in the table.
  • Selection Limit — configures the maximum limit of the number of rows that can be selected at once (e.g., by using Select All).
  • Threshold — determines the maximum number of entries loaded when the application is started. Scroll Threshold determines the maximum number loaded during scrolling if new entries need to be fetched. Increasing these settings will lead to an increased initial load time but deliver a smoother scroll experience.

Note: Not all settings listed above are available for all table types and page types. Furthermore, there are differences between Fiori elements V2 and Fiori elements V4 (Fiori elements V2 offers fewer settings).

Example – List Report Grid Table in Fiori elements V4:

 

 

New feature: configuration for the filter bar

The Configuration context menu entry on the filter bar opens settings for the filter bar. Among other things, the following settings can be configured here:

  • Display Clear Button — configures whether the Clear button is displayed in the filter bar.
  • Enable Semantic Date Range menu options — configures that single date range filters offer semantic operators (like "Today", "This Month", "Last Year").

Example – List Report Filter Bar in Fiori elements V4:

  

 

New feature: configuration for the complete list report / object page

The Configuration context menu entry at page level opens settings for the entire List Report or Object Page. Among other things, the following settings can be configured here:

  • Variant Management (Table Variant Management) — configures how variant management is used on the List Report page or in tables on the Object Page.
  • Initial Load — configures how data is loaded initially when the app is opened.

Example – List Report in Fiori elements V4:

 

 

New feature: change text arrangement

The Change Text Arrangement functionality is available in key user adaptation mode when the context menu entry Change Text Arrangement is selected on a table, on the filter bar, or on a form.

This powerful new functionality allows changing centrally the text arrangement for all relevant fields in a Fiori app. A key user can decide for each field whether it shall be shown as:

Option

Example (Company Code "0001" / "SAP AG")

ID only

0001

Text only

SAP AG

Text first

SAP AG (0001)

Text last

0001 (SAP AG)

Note: These changes are independent of specific controls. You change the text arrangement for the underlying OData property. If a specific field or control does not use the text arrangement from the OData property, the changes will not be applied to that control.

 

 

New feature: change labels

The Change Labels functionality is available in key user adaptation mode when the context menu entry Change Labels is selected on a table, on the filter bar, or on a form.

This allows changing the labels centrally for all relevant fields in a Fiori app: if an identical label is available in the filter bar, in tables, forms, and charts of a Fiori application, then all places are changed consistently. This is the key difference from the existing Rename feature, which only applies locally to a form field.

Example: You can consistently change the text "Material" to "Product" across your entire Fiori app.

Note: These changes are independent of specific controls. You change the label for the underlying OData property. If a specific field or control does not use the text from the OData property, the changes will not be applied to that control.

 

 

Need more complex changes? Seamless Transition from Key User to Developer Extensibility.

If you need to make more complex changes — for example, adding fragments to visualize data as a chart, changing a responsive table to a grid table, or making the app available to different business roles, user groups, organizational units, and countries — you can extend an application by creating an SAPUI5 Adaptation Project (developer adaptation).

When creating an SAPUI5 Adaptation Project, you can select the brand-new option Import Key User Changes (available since S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602; not yet available for S/4HANA Private Cloud / On-Premise) to import all key user changes made so far. This means key user changes will not be lost — they become part of the new SAPUI5 Adaptation Project.

 

For an introduction to Adaptation Projects, see Work with SAPUI5 Adaptation Projects to Make Changes and Extend the Source Code of an Application Variant of an SAP Fiori Application in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

For more detailed information, see Extending SAP Fiori Applications.

 

Prerequisites

Public Cloud:

  • The IAM app F5933_LRST_TRAN provides the relevant authorizations. It is included in the business catalog SAP_CORE_BC_EXT_FLX_CUS_PC (see Adapting SAP Fiori UIs at Runtime - Key User Adaptation).

Private Cloud / On-Premise:

  • Your administrator assigned you to the authorization role SAP_UI_FLEX_KEY_USER (see Enabling Key User Adaptation).

General requirements:

  • You run your SAPUI5 app on a desktop computer or laptop.
  • Your app is enabled for key user adaptation.
  • A minimum screen size of 800×600 is required for key user adaptation. Anything smaller can affect the toolbar elements, causing them to overlap.

 

Tips for Key User Adaptation for Fiori Apps in SAP S/4HANA

Where do I make Key User changes?

  • Key users typically make changes in the Development environment.

How do I transport Key User changes?

  • Active changes must be published to make them ready for transport.
  • To publish your changes for transport, you must be assigned a transport request by your technical team.
  • Your technical team manages the transports through to your Production environment.

 

Key takeaways

  • Key User Adaptation lets you make common changes to SAP Fiori apps directly in the Fiori Launchpad — no developer knowledge required.
  • Key User Adaptation is now extremely powerful with new options for:
    • Configuring important UI elements (tables, filter bar, complete list report / object page)
    • Changing the text arrangement of fields
    • Changing labels globally across an entire Fiori app
  • Changes are made in the Development environment and published for transport.
  • Published changes can be transported to other environments by your technical team.
  • Key User Adaptation changes can be imported when creating an SAPUI5 Adaptation Project for more complex changes (S/4HANA Public Cloud 2602+). This allows a seamless transition from key user to developer extensibility.

 

Where can I learn more about Key User Adaptation for Fiori Apps in SAP S/4HANA?

  • SAP Help – Key User Adaptation (S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition): Adapting SAP Fiori UIs at Runtime – Key User Adaptation
  • SAP Help – Key User Adaptation (S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and On-Premise): Adapting SAP Fiori UIs at Runtime – Key User Adaptation
  • SAP Learning – Tutorials: Key User Adaptation for SAPUI5 Applications
  • SAP Help – SAP Fiori elements V4: Extending Delivered Apps With Key User Adaptation
  • SAP Help – SAP Fiori elements V2: Adapting the UI

entry.title: Building a Scalable Micro-Frontend Dashboard with SAPUI5 Integration Cards
entry.content:
Introduction

Building an enterprise-grade analytics dashboard is challenging. When you add requirements like multi-team development, independent deployment cycles, and the need for extensibility, traditional monolithic approaches quickly become unmanageable.

This article shares our proposal building the Performance Dashboard at SAP—a micro-frontend architecture that supports dozens of independent KPI widgets, dynamic filtering, cross-widget interactions, and continuous deployment—all while maintaining team autonomy and code quality.

This article shares practical patterns for building a micro-frontend dashboard architecture that supports:

  • Dozens of independent KPI widgets
  • Dynamic filtering across all widgets
  • Cross-widget interactions
  • Continuous deployment with team autonomy

Whether you're building analytics dashboards, composable UIs, or exploring micro-frontend patterns, this deep-dive offers practical insights backed by real-world architectural decisions.

The Challenge: Complex Dashboard, Multiple Teams

An enterprise analytics dashboard that provides managers with real-time visibility into business metrics across multiple dimensions:

Requirements:

  • 40+ KPI widgets across multiple views × multiple dimensions
  • Multiple teams developing widgets independently
  • Continuous deployment without blocking other teams
  • Dynamic filters affecting all widgets simultaneously
  • Cross-filtering when users click chart segments
  • Grid customization (drag-drop, resize, hide/show)

Why Traditional Approaches Fail:

  • Monolithic SPA: Every widget change requires full redeployment
  • Tight coupling: One broken widget blocks entire dashboard
  • Single pipeline: Multiple teams competing for deployment window

We needed true micro-frontend architecture.

 

Architecture Decision 1: Repository Structure

The Question

Should the dashboard shell and KPI widgets live in one repository or separate repositories?

Option A: Monorepo

analytics-dashboard/          ← Single repo
├── dashboard-shell/
│   ├── webapp/
│   └── ui5.yaml
└── widgets/
    ├── widget-a/
    ├── widget-b/
    └── ...

Pros: Simple setup, single CI/CD pipeline
Cons: Deployment coupling, merge conflicts, repository grows massive

 

Option B: Two Repositories - Selected

dashboard-shell/           ← Repo 1: Orchestration layer
├── webapp/
│   ├── config/layout.json
│   ├── controller/Dashboard.controller.ts
│   ├── model/LayoutManager.ts
│   └── view/Dashboard.view.xml

widget-registry/          ← Repo 2: All widgets
├── widgets/
│   ├── widget-a/
│   ├── widget-b/
│   └── ...
├── registry-service/
│   └── widget-registry.json
└── build.sh

Contract Between Repositories: Repository Exposes Consumes

RepositoryExposesConsumes
widget-registry

GET /api/widget-registry

GET /[widgetName]/*

-
dashboard-shell

-

Discovers widgets

Loads assets dynamically

Why We Chose Two Repositories:

Independent deployment cycles:

  • Dashboard updates don't require widget releases
  • Widget teams deploy on their own schedule
  • Broken widget doesn't block dashboard

Clear ownership boundaries

  • Dashboard team owns orchestration
  • Widget teams own visualizations
  • Contract enforces decoupling

Scalable CI/CD

  • Two focused pipelines vs one massive pipeline
  • Faster builds (only changed repo rebuilds)
  • Parallel development without conflicts

Trade-off: Slightly more complex integration testing, but worth it for deployment flexibility.

 

Architecture Decision 2: Integration Strategy

The Question

How should the dashboard load and render independent widgets at runtime?

Evaluated Options

Option A: Iframe-Based 

<iframe src="https://widgets.example.com/widget-a"></iframe>

Pros:Complete isolation
Cons: Performance overhead, difficult communication, styling inconsistency, accessibility issues

 

Option B: Native Web Components

customElements.define('analytics-widget', class extends HTMLElement {});

Pros: Browser-native, framework-agnostic
Cons: No SAPUI5 features, reinvent data binding/routing/i18n, steep learning curve

 

Option C: UI5 Components

import Component from "sap/ui/core/Component";

const oComponent = await Component.create({ name: "com.example.widget" });

Pros: Full SAPUI5 capabilities
Cons: Complex lifecycle management, no standard metadata format

 

Option D : UI5 Integration Cards - Selected

Manifest-based configuration:

{
    "sap.app": {
        "id": "com.example.ordervalue",
        "type": "card"
    },
    "sap.card": {
        "type": "Component",
        "header": {
            "title": "Order Value at Risk"
        },
        "content": {
            "componentName": "com.example.ordervalue"
        }
    }
}

Dashboard loads dynamically:

import Card from "sap/ui/integration/widgets/Card";

const oCard = new Card({
    manifest: "https://widgets.example.com/kpi-widget1/manifest.json",
    baseUrl: "https://widgets.example.com/kpi-widget1"
});
oCard.placeAt("container");

 

Why Integration Cards: 

Standardized format - Official SAPUI5 specification

Support Two card types:

  • Component cards: Full custom code for complex widgets
  • Declarative cards: Manifest-only (List, Table, Analytical, ...)

Built-in features: Headers, data binding, actions, error handling

Parameter passing: card.setParameters() with auto-refresh

Framework alignment: Native SAPUI5, team expertise, Fiori guidelines

 

Architecture Decision 3: Communication Patterns

The Challenge: Bidirectional Communication

Two-way communication required:

  1. Dashboard → Widgets: Apply filters to all widgets
  2. Widgets → Dashboard: Handle user clicks (cross-filtering, navigation)

 

Evaluated Patterns

Pattern 1: Pure EventBus

import EventBus from "sap/ui/core/EventBus";

// SAP UI5 EventBus (pub/sub communication)
const eventBus = EventBus.getInstance();

eventBus.publish("filters", "changed", {
  timeRange: "last7days" // For example
});

Pros: Loose coupling
Cons: State scattered in events

 

Pattern 2: State-Driven Reactive 

import JSONModel from "sap/ui/model/json/JSONModel";

// SAP UI5 JSON Model (client-side state)
const filterModel = new JSONModel({
  timeRange: "last7days"
});

Pros: Single source of truth
Cons: Integration Cards don't support reactive binding

 

Pattern 3: Host-Controlled

import Card from "sap/ui/integration/widgets/Card";

// SAP UI5 Integration Cards - trigger data refresh
cards.forEach((card) => card.refresh());

Pros: Centralized control
Cons: Tight coupling

 

Our Solution: Hybrid EventBus + State-Driven

Best of both worlds:

import JSONModel from "sap/ui/model/json/JSONModel";
import EventBus from "sap/ui/core/EventBus";

// SAP UI5 EventBus instance
const eventBus = EventBus.getInstance();

/**
 * 1. Centralized State (Single Source of Truth)
 * Shared JSONModel for filter state across the application
 */
const filterModel = new JSONModel({
  timeRange: null,
  category: [],
  status: []
});

this.getView().setModel(filterModel, "filters");


/**
 * 2. FilterBar → Update State + Publish Event
 */
onSearch() {
  const filters = this.getFilterValues();

  // Update centralized state
  this.getModel("filters").setData(filters);

  // Notify other components (no payload coupling)
  eventBus.publish("app.filters", "changed", {
    timestamp: new Date().toISOString()
  });
}


/**
 * 3. Dashboard → React to State Change
 */
onFiltersChanged() {

  // Always read from centralized state (source of truth)
  const filters = this.getModel("filters").getData();

  // Update all Integration Cards (widgets)
  this._cards.forEach((card) => {
    card.setParameters(filters);
    card.refresh(); // triggers data re-fetch + re-render
  });
}

Pros:

Single Source of Truth: Centralized filter state

Explicit Notifications: EventBus for change observation

Universal Support: Works with Component and Declarative cards

Debuggable: Inspect model state + trace events

Testable: Mock state and events independently

 

Widget-to-Dashboard Communication

Widgets fire action events on user interaction

{
  "sap.card": {
    "type": "List",
    "content": {
      "item": {
        "title": "{name}",
        "actions": [
          {
            "type": "Custom",
            "parameters": {
              "action": "viewDetails",
              "id": "{id}",
              "name": "{name}"
            }
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}

Host (Dashboard) handle it

oCard.attachAction((oEvent) => {
  const action = oEvent.getParameter("action");
  const params = oEvent.getParameter("parameters");

  if (action === "Custom" && params.action === "viewDetails") {
    // handle widget interaction
    console.log("Item clicked:", params);

    eventBus.publish("widget.item", "selected", params);
  }
});

Flow:

  1. User clicks item in widget
  2. Widget fires action event
  3. Dashboard receives event
  4. Dashboard updates filter state
  5. Dashboard publishes filter change
  6. All widgets refresh with new filter

 

Lessons Learned

What Worked Well

Two-repository approach

  • True team independence
  • No merge conflicts

Deploy anytime without coordination

SAPUI5 Integration Cards

  • Standardized pattern
  • Supports Component and Declarative types
  • Built-in features reduce boilerplate

Hybrid communication

  • Centralized state = single source of truth
  • EventBus = loose coupling
  • Works with all card types

Contract-based development

  • Registry acts as interface definition
  • Tags enable dynamic filtering
  • Clear responsibilities

 

Results & Key Takeaways

Achieved:

  • 40+ widgets by 10+ teams
  • Independent deployment cycles
  • Zero downtime deployments

Key Insights:

  • Micro-frontends are organizational—they're about team autonomy and deployment independence, not just technology.
  • Match patterns to constraints—we chose Integration Cards because teams knew UI5, not because it's universally "best."
  • Centralize wisely—filters are centralized (single source of truth), implementation is distributed (team autonomy).
  • Contracts are your API—treat the widget registry with the same rigor as REST APIs.

 

Resources

SAPUI5 Documentation:

  • UI5 Integration Cards
  • EventBus API
  • Component Cards

Micro-frontend Patterns:

  • Micro Frontends by Martin Fowler

entry.title: Running Hugging Face AI Models with SAPUI5 and CAPM
entry.content:
Hi everyone !

After spending several hours researching and experimenting, I finally found a simple way to answer a question that many SAP developers have started asking:

  “Is it possible to run AI models inside SAP applications using SAPUI5 and CAPM?”

The short answer is:

   Yes, absolutely!

In this blog, I will share how we can integrate Hugging Face AI models with SAPUI5 and CAPM, and how we can build a simple AI-powered application that can generate text responses and even images.

First Question: What is Hugging Face?

 If you are new to AI development, you might wonder:

What exactly is Hugging Face?

Think of Hugging Face as GitHub for AI models.

It is a platform where developers and researchers publish and share machine learning models.

Currently, Hugging Face provides access to hundreds of thousands of AI models that can perform tasks such as:

  • Text generation
  • Chatbots
  • Image generation
  • Speech recognition
  • Translation
  • Code generation

The best part is that you can use these models directly through APIs without training your own model.

Another great advantage is that Hugging Face provides GPU infrastructure, which means you can run advanced models without owning expensive hardware.

And yes — if you build your own model, you can also publish it on Hugging Face and make it available globally.

        Fig 1.1 Hugging Face Models sections

 

Now the Interesting Part: Can SAPUI5 Use AI?

When I started this experiment, my first question was:

Can SAPUI5 directly call an AI model?

The answer is yes, especially for text generation models.

To make this work, we only need three things:

-  A Hugging Face account
-  An HF API Token
-  A model endpoint

Once you log in to Hugging Face, you can generate an HF Token from your account settings.

Then you can choose any model depending on your use case. Hugging Face provides model benchmarks and descriptions, so you can easily decide which one is best for:

  • Education
  • Coding
  • Reasoning
  • Chat
  • Content generation

Connecting Hugging Face with SAPUI5

Now let's talk about the implementation.

In the SAPUI5 controller, we can send a request to the Hugging Face API using JavaScript fetch().

The UI simply takes a user prompt, sends it to the AI model, and displays the response.

Example flow:

User enters a prompt:

       "What is Hugging Face and SAPUI5?"

SAPUI5 sends the prompt to the Hugging Face model.

The model generates a response and returns it.

Then SAPUI5 displays the result directly on the UI.

This makes it very easy to build AI chat assistants inside SAP applications.

         Fig 1.2 Output- Hugging Face Ai Chat

 

Sample Code:

      Fig 1.3 sapui5 Controller.js - Hugging Face Ai Chat

 

        Fig 1.3 sapui5 view.xml - Hugging Face Ai Chat

 

But What About Image Generation?

This is where things become interesting.

When I tried running image generation models directly in SAPUI5, I realized something important.

⚠️SAPUI5 works best with text-based AI APIs.

For image generation models, we need a backend service.

This is where CAPM (Cloud Application Programming Model) becomes very useful.

 

 

Using CAPM for Image Generation

To generate images, we can integrate fal.ai, which provides inference APIs for advanced image generation models.

Some examples include:

  • black-forest-labs / FLUX.1-dev
  • Tongyi-MAI / Z-Image-Turbo

These models can generate high-quality AI images from text prompts.

For example:

"Portrait of a futuristic astronaut with glowing blue eyes"

The system sends this prompt to the AI model, and the model generates an image.

 

How CAPM Handles This

Inside the CAPM Node.js service, we create a custom action such as:

     "generateImage"

This action receives the prompt from SAPUI5.

Then CAPM calls the fal.ai API with that prompt.

The AI model generates the image and returns a URL of the generated image, which we send back to the UI.

This makes CAPM act as a bridge between SAPUI5 and AI models.

 

Why Use fal.ai Instead of Hugging Face Directly?

You might ask:

       If we already use Hugging Face, why do we need fal.ai?

          Good question.

Some models listed on Hugging Face are actually hosted through different inference providers, including fal.ai.

For example:

black-forest-labs / FLUX.1-dev

This model is often executed through fal.ai inference APIs.

So fal.ai becomes an easy way to run those models with strong GPU performance.

Sample Code:

 

Fig 1.4 Output – CAPM - Hugging face Image generation

 

Fig 1.5 service.js - first half- Hugging face Image generation

 

Fig 1.6 service.js - second half - Hugging face Image generation

 

Fig 1.7 Fal.ai API key Generation - Hugging face Image generation

 

Final Result

After connecting everything together, we get a simple AI application:

The application can:

- Generate AI text responses using SAPUI5
- Generate AI images using CAPM services
- Use Hugging Face models
- Run without training your own model

All of this can be done using free developer APIs, unless you need large-scale GPU workloads.

 

Conclusion

From this experiment, we can see that SAP applications can easily integrate with modern AI platforms.

The architecture becomes very simple:

                   SAPUI5
                        ⬇
           CAPM Service
                      ⬇
   Hugging Face / fal.ai AI Models

 

Using this approach, developers can build powerful applications such as:

  • AI chat assistants inside SAP
  • AI-powered knowledge systems
  • Image generation tools
  • Smart enterprise automation

And the best part is that you can even deploy your own AI model on Hugging Face and use it inside SAP applications.

 

I hope this small experiment helps other developers who are curious about AI + SAP integration.

Happy coding!

entry.title: SAP User Experience Q1/2026 Update – Part 9: UI Design and Technology for Web and Mobile
entry.content:
This ninth and last post in my series gives an overview innovations in our UI tools and technologies, making it ever easier to design, build and integrate UIs, for both web and mobile applications. For designing apps, web and mobile, we have added AI content and more to the SAP Design System portal and UI Kits in Figma. For web developers, we have introduced the SAP Fiori development portal, as well as more AI support via MCP servers for SAPUI5 and for SAP Fiori elements, and Joule integration. Beyond that: have a look at how you can use AI to migrate to TypeScript, learn about the new Geomap control, and benefit from the new SAPUI5 runtime repository. Finally, discover the latest updates for mobile design and development with our SDKs and the mobile development kit.

Joule can also be seen as a technology for delivering a great user experience. To find out about recent innovations there, as well as for central entry points and services, have a look at this post, which also contains links to all the other parts of the series:

  • SAP UX Q3/2025 Update – Part 1: AI, Joule, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Mobile Start.

In case you missed them, do have a look at my previous posts covering design and technology news, since what I mention here builds partly on what came before:

  • SAP User Experience Q1/2025 Update – Part 7: UI Design and Technology for Web and Mobile. This post has a lot of information about extensibility, especially for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Do also have a look at the new SAP Extensibility Explorer.
  • SAP UX Q3/2025 Update – Part 7: UI Design and Technology for Web and Mobile.

First, we’ll have a look at news for creating web UIs, before looking at what’s new for mobile design and development.

 

News for Creating Web UIs

I’ve compiled the news into these sections:

  • SAP Design System Portal and UI Kits – now with AI UI Elements.
  • SAP Fiori Development Portal.
  • Using AI to Develop UIs – MCP Servers, Joule.
  • SAP Fiori Tools News.

SAP Design System Portal and UI Kits – now with AI UI Elements

We recently introduced the new SAP Design System portal that brings together the product design guidelines SAP Fiori for web, SAP Fiori for iOS, and SAP Fiori for Android, as well as to the new digital design system guidelines. With its enhanced usability, simplified versioning and improved performance, designers (and developers!) get a better user experience compared with the previous SAP Fiori design guidelines.

End of last year we introduced these AI UI elements for web UIs, as you can see in Figure 1 below:

  • AI Acknowledgement.
  • AI Button.
  • AI Writing Assistant.

Figure 1: AI UI elements available in SAP Design System portal. Alt Text: Screenshot of the portal UI Elements page with a filter at the top containing “AI”, below that “3 results” showing three cards in a horizontal row for “AI Acknowledgement”, “AI Button” and “AI Writing Assistant”.

Get a complete overview of what’s new in the design system portal here

  • What’s New in the SAP Design System Portal?

UI Kits v8.0 and v9.0 – now with AI components

The new UI Kits available for designers in Figma make it easy for designers to design accessible UIs at enterprise scale, for web applications, mobile iOS and Android applications as well as wearables. The UI Kits provide reusable components for web and mobile, along with web floorplans, which give you what you need out-of-the-box.

In the meantime, we have released SAP Web UI Kit v8.0 which includes a full set of AI components: AI Button, AI Menu Button, AI Split Menu Button, AI Input, and AI Text Area.

The latest release, SAP Web UI Kit v9.0, continues to evolve the system with focused improvements that enhance visual quality, usability, and overall consistency across the kit. It brings:

  • Updated Icons:
    All icons have been refreshed with a 1.5px stroke (down from 1.6px). This improves anti-aliasing and results in cleaner, sharper visuals across interfaces.
  • Busy Indicator Enhancements:
    You now have two Busy Indicator options, static and animated, available on the same page. The new animated version is ready to use out of the box, saving time and implementation effort.
  • Redesigned Color Palette. Note that the previous color palette component has been removed in order to avoid confusion, and keep the kit cleaner and easier to navigate.

Find out more here:

  • New SAP Design System Portal for SAP Fiori Design Guidelines, and New UI Kits for Designers.

SAP Fiori Development Portal

I’m also happy to inform you about the new SAP Fiori development portal. It is designed to help developers maximize the benefits of SAP Fiori elements for OData V4, which is more than just a UI framework: it’s a metadata-driven approach that leverages the SAP Design System as its guiding principle, incorporating the most relevant design system options for typical business tasks.

Figure 2: The SAP Fiori development portal. Alt text: A screenshot showing the Introduction page. On the left a menu has entries Using the Portal, Building Blocks, Global Patterns, Floorplans, SAP Fiori Tools. The Introduction page has a full width card “Building Blocks” at the top, with a description and tiles representing various building blocks. Below that “Choose Your Option” heading above two side-by-side cards for “Floorplans” and “Customer Layout”, each with an image and description, and a “Learn More” button.

The SAP Fiori development portal puts building blocks front and center. These building blocks - such as tables, forms, filter bars, and charts - are orchestrated by SAP Fiori elements and form the basis of every OData V4 application. They provide standardized layouts and interaction patterns, ensuring UI consistency and boosting developer productivity. The great advantage of using building blocks is that you can use them to create a customer layout and yet still benefit from the advantages of the metadata driven approach: low code, built in enterprise-grade capabilities such as multi-language support, accessibility, security and performance, and design system consistency.

Find out more in this six-part series of blog posts, here is the first one, which contains links to the others:

  • Introducing the SAP Fiori Development Portal: Your Gateway to Rapid SAP Fiori App Creation (1 of 6).

We have also restructured our documentation for SAP Fiori elements, with separate entry points for OData V4 and OData V2, and better supporting the building block approach. Read more here:

  • SAP Fiori Elements: New Documentation Structure.

Using AI to Develop UIs – MCP Servers, Joule

The above posts from Q1/2025 and Q3/2025 introduced a number of powerful ways to accelerate UI development by using AI, such as:

  • Creating SAP Fiori elements apps from a sketch (in the Q1 post), now integrated in SAP Build Code and Joule (Q3 post)
  • Joule for SAPUI5 freestyle development (Q1 post)
  • AI-assisted auto-fix of coding errors with UI5 linter (Q3 post)

Recently, we have gone even further and introduced MCP servers for freestyle SAPUI5 development as well as for developing with SAP Fiori elements and the associated building blocks. These open-source Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers provide AI agents with comprehensive UI5 and SAP Fiori elements knowledge, including best practices and project-aware context information, and thus provide developers with valuable support. Figure 3 shows examples.

Figure 3: Examples of the UI5 MCP Server (on the left) and the MCP Server for SAP Fiori (on the right) in action. Alt Text: both images show screenshots of a CLINE console view with a task entered as text at the top, followed by a list of steps which the system proposes to do to solve the task.

Read about these new MCP servers in these blog posts:

  • UI5 MCP Server.
  • MCP Server for SAP Fiori.
  • Setting the right expectations around AI for code generation and getting past the inherent limits

Alternatively, you can listen to these podcasts involving Tim Back and Florian Vogt, talking about the intricacies of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) within the SAP ecosystem:

  • Spotify: UI5 & MCP - Navigating the Model Context Protocol in SAP.
  • Apple Podcast: UI5 & MCP - Navigating the Model Context Protocol in SAP.

Latest UI5 MCP Server Enhancements

The UI5 MCP server got extended to help migrate existing JavaScript code to TypeScript, as well as for generating UI Integration Cards.

New TypeScript Migration Feature

With the newest feature of the UI5 MCP server, UI5-specific TypeScript conversion assistance is now available to AI coding agents, making the task much easier. Find out more in this blog post:

  • UI5 MCP Server's New TypeScript Migration Feature - Real-World Experience and Best Practices

Generating UI Integration Cards

The UI5 MCP Server now supports the development of UI Integration Cards. In its latest version – v0.2.1 – it includes three new tools dedicated to UI Integration Cards:

  1. Best practice guidance for your agent.
  2. Scaffolding a new Integration Card.
  3. Validating the Card’s manifest.

These tools assist Large Language Models (LLMs) in creating new cards from scratch or editing existing ones, following the best practices for card development. Find out more here:

  • UI5 MCP Server Got Extended for UI Integration Cards Generation.

AI News for SAP Fiori Tools and MCP Server for SAP Fiori

The Project Accelerator can now be launched directly from the SAP Fiori generator, streamlining the initial project creation flow. The previously required staging step has been removed, allowing new projects to be created directly in the selected workspace folder. For projects created using the Project Accelerator, the Application Modeler now offers advanced editing capabilities, such as creating new fields and properties end-to-end, updating the data model, exposing the service, and consuming the changes in the UI in one coherent flow.

AI support has also been extended to mock data generation. Developers can generate realistic mock data using AI directly in the Application Modeler, and this capability is available for all applications whenever the Project Accelerator is enabled.

In parallel, the MCP server for SAP Fiori continues to expand. The server now exposes @sap-ux/create commands as CLI functionality, enabling reliable execution in MCP-supported workflows. App generation has been extended to support CAP Java projects, and a new “search_docs” tool allows the LLM (Large Language Model) to query SAP Fiori elements documentation, annotations, SAP Fiori tools documentation, and SAP Fiori Development Portal resources to quickly find relevant guidance and code snippets.

Joule Enhancement: Create Freestyle SAPUI5 Apps in SAP Build Code

You can now use Joule in SAP Build Code to create new freestyle SAPUI5 apps according to your requirements. The feature is not absolutely groundbreaking, as there are other tools doing a similar job like the Easy-UI5 templates and the recently published UI5 MCP Server. But:

  1. It brings the functionality right to the fingertips of developers using SAP Build Code – no other tools needed.
  2. It extends the template capabilities beyond what the Easy-UI5 templates provide.
  3. Implementing it within the Build Code Joule environment yielded interesting insights and further improvements.

Find out more about the feature, and in the second “making of” post, read about how Andreas Kunz went about building it, and what he learnt from doing it:

  • Joule in SAP Build Code can now create freestyle UI5 apps.
  • Inside Joule: Lessons from building an AI coding assistant for SAP Build.

SAP Fiori Tools News

In addition to the AI-enhancements mentioned above, we now provide expanded support for SAP Fiori elements building blocks, a new connection manager for SAP Systems in VS Code, and a feature to preview SAP Fiori web apps on mobile devices.

Expanded Support for SAP Fiori Elements Building Blocks

The following enhancements make it easier to adapt applications and allow developers to work more confidently and efficiently with SAP Fiori elements–based UIs.

Developers can now add more building block types to their apps with the Page Editor, including

  • rich text editors within custom sections,
  • custom columns for table building blocks, and
  • the page building block itself.

Managing custom pages is also more intuitive, as building blocks can now be deleted directly from the editor.

To support experimentation and iteration, undo and redo actions are now available when working on custom pages.

New Connection Manager for SAP Systems in VS Code

We have introduced the Connection Manager for SAP Systems as a dedicated Visual Studio Code extension for managing connections to remote SAP systems. The Connection Manager allows developers to create, store, test, and edit connection information that can be reused by the SAP Fiori generator as well as by preview features in SAP Fiori tools.

Preview SAP Fiori Web Apps on Mobile

When developing SAP Fiori applications, simulating a mobile experience in a desktop browser often isn’t enough to fully validate real-world behavior. Touch interactions, performance characteristics, and device-specific rendering can differ noticeably on physical phones and tablets.

To address this, SAP Fiori tools now supports previewing applications directly on real mobile devices. You do this by using @sap/ux-ui5-tooling version 1.19.1 or higher and creating a new run configuration and enabling Remote Access. When you start the run configuration, the terminal provides connection details, including a URL and a QR code, which can be opened directly on a phone or tablet to access the running app.

Find out more here:

  • SAP Fiori tools 2601 update – Mobile Device Preview, Building Blocks, and AI-Driven Productivity.
  • SAP Fiori Development newsletter January 2026 (issue #38).
  • SAP Fiori Development newsletter issue #39 (March 2026).

 

News for Mobile Designers and Developers

Let’s first look at updates in the design guidelines and the SDKs, and then look at news for the mobile development kit (MDK).

Mobile Design Guideline and SDK Updates

We have introduced numerous new and enhanced patterns and components this last half-year. I have selected a small number to highlight here, further below you’ll find links to get the full overview.

New for AI

The following two new patterns have been introduced for both the SAP Fiori for Android and the SAP Fiori for iOS design guidelines.

AI Insights Pattern: We have introduced a new AI Insights Pattern, which describes how to present AI insights optimally on mobile devices, as shown in Figure 4. It provides a standardized way to display a summary, analysis, or suggestion generated with AI. This pattern can be applied to a full-screen or a card depending on the scope of content generated by AI.

AI Handoff Pattern: We have created a new guideline describing the AI Handoff Pattern, which defines how a user should transition between contextual embedded AI features and the Joule conversational panel.

Figure 4: Two new AI patterns: AI Insights Pattern and AI Handoff Pattern. Alt Text: On the left two image for AI Insights Pattern, first a full mobile phone screen with title and subtitle at the top, followed by two insights sections, each with two slots for content, at the bottom a user feedback row with thumbs up and thumbs down icons, above a an AI generate button. On the right a diagram for the AI Handoff Pattern showing a schematic mobile module with arrow to and from a schematic Joule UI.

Further news

I have picked two stand-out updates, also made available for Android and iOS.

New Illustrations: We have introduced the new illustrations, that were also introduced in the web, to the mobile design system and the mobile SDKs. You can see a few examples on the left in Figure 5 below.

More flexible card layouts: We offer far more flexibility now in how you use cards to summarize information. For example, you can create wider cards that have the image on the left or the right, and the content block next to it. With the new flex item component, the card’s main header can now be customized to hold more content by adding tags and status info labels outside, on top, between, or below the title and subtitle. Two examples are shown in Figure 5 on the right.

Figure 5: New illustrations and more flexible card layout and content options. Alt Text: On the left three different-size “My Daily Tasks” cards, with images for “You’r all caught up”, “Oops” and “Congratulations”. On the right two card examples: at the top a slim wide card with title, subtitle and one line text on the left, image on the right; below that a larger card with image on the left, title and details with colored indicators and buttons on the right.

Find out more here:

  • Introducing SAP Fiori for Android/iOS 25.8: Innovative Enhancements for Android and iOS.

For an overview of all updates, have a look at the what’s new sections in the SAP Design System portal and the SAP Mobile Services Documentation

  • SAP Fiori for Android design: What's New for SAP BTP SDK for Android 26.1 and older.
  • SAP BTP SDK for Android: What’s New in Release 26.1.2 and earlier releases.
  • SAP Fiori for iOS design: What's New for SAP BTP SDK for iOS 26.1 and older.
  • SAP BTP SDK for iOS: What’s New in Version 26.1.2 and earlier releases.

Mobile Development Kit (MDK) News

The Mobile Development Kit, available as part of SAP Mobile Services, enables developers and technical business users to build cross-platform mobile applications. It allows you to build your application once, in an integrated development environment (SAP Business Application Studio / VSCode extension) and run it natively on Mobile Android and iOS devices.  

Here I have chosen to highlight one area: enhancements to the calander control, which now supports date range selection as well as allowing you to highlight special events or statuses directly on the calendar.

The new date range selection displays the calendar in a month-view format and allows multiple months to be shown. Users can scroll vertically through the calendar months. This is ideal if you need to plan events or manage schedules over extended periods, since users can select a time period with a start date and an end date (see Figure 6 below):

  • The first date selected becomes the start date of the range.
  • The second date selected becomes the end date of the range.

Figure 6: Date range selection supported by the mobile development kit provided by SAP Mobile Service. Alt Text: An image of an iPhone and an Android phone next to each other. The iPhone with the light Morning Horizon theme and a date selection Sep 16 – Sep 29, 2025, the other with the dark Evening Horizon theme and a date selection Mon Sep 29 to Fri Oct 17 2025.

Find out more here:

  • Blog post: What’s new in Mobile development kit client 25.9.
  • Documentation: What’s New in Mobile Development Kit 25.9.1 and earlier releases.

 

Further Information

For creating web applications, do register for the upcoming UI5con virtual conference.

Learn more about it at https://openui5.org/ui5con/faq.html, including how to attend, sponsor, or propose a topic for a session.

For links to design and development tools and technology, have a look at the SAP Fiori topic page in the SAP Community, or go directly to these pages:

  • SAP Design System.
  • SAP Fiori tools.
  • SAPUI5.
  • SAP Fiori elements.
  • SAP Mobile technologies.

 

Final words

As you can see from all seven posts in the series, we continue to progress rapidly with the user experience of our product portfolio, especially with regards to AI-assistance.

Part 1 of the series lists all the parts, and links to those parts that have already been published:

  • SAP UX Q1/2026 Update – Part 1: AI, Joule, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Mobile Start.

Do keep posting your experiences and recommendations yourself in the SAP Community, with the SAP Fiori and/or the User Experience tag! In case you are wondering how to get a list of the most recent blog posts on SAP Fiori and User Experience in our new SAP Community, use these links:

  • Latest blog posts on SAP Fiori in the SAP Community.
  • Latest blog posts on User Experience in the SAP Community.

For general information on design at SAP and SAP Fiori, check out:

  • www.sap.com/design.
  • www.sap.com/fiori.
  • SAP Community: SAP Fiori topic page.

entry.title: Removal of legacy sap.ui.webc.* Web Component UI libraries from SAPUI5/OpenUI5 1.147.0 onwards
entry.content:
Saying Goodbye to sap.ui.webc.* — How SAPUI5/OpenUI5 Now Integrates Web Components Natively

If you've used the sap.ui.webc.main, sap.ui.webc.fiori, or sap.ui.webc.common libraries in a SAPUI5 or OpenUI5 application, this post is for you. These libraries will be removed in version 1.147.0, one release before the next long-term support (LTS) version 1.148. They will not be available in the 1.148 LTS or any version after.

Here's what happened, what's changing, and what you should do.

The Short Version

The sap.ui.webc.* libraries were an experimental attempt to bring UI5 Web Components into the SAPUI5 programming model. They were introduced in version 1.92 (2021) as experimental, deprecated in version 1.120 (2023), and will be removed in version 1.147.0. They never left experimental status.

The new solution is a much better approach: native Web Components integration built directly into sap.ui.core, available since version 1.138. Instead of frozen wrapper libraries, you now consume Web Components from npm as first-class citizens of the framework.

What Were the sap.ui.webc.* Libraries?

The idea was straightforward: take each UI5 Web Component (like ui5-button, ui5-shellbar, ui5-table) and wrap it as a classic SAPUI5 ManagedObject-based control — complete with properties, aggregations, events, and data binding support.

Three libraries made up this approach:

Library Purpose

sap.ui.webc.commonBase infrastructure, bundled the UI5 Web Components runtime
sap.ui.webc.main~70 controls wrapping @ui5/webcomponents (Button, Input, Table, Dialog, ...)
sap.ui.webc.fiori~30 controls wrapping @ui5/webcomponents-fiori (ShellBar, Wizard, Timeline, ...)

This "retrofit" approach worked, but it came with significant drawbacks:

  • Frozen dependency. The libraries bundled @ui5/webcomponents v1.18.0 as static thirdparty files. When the UI5 Web Components project shipped v2.x with major improvements, the sap.ui.webc.* libraries stayed on v1.18 — forever.
  • Maintenance overhead. Every new Web Component or API change required manually updating the wrapper code and re-bundling the thirdparty files. This didn't scale.
  • Experimental lock. Because of these constraints, the libraries never graduated beyond @experimental status. They were always flagged with: "This API is experimental and might change significantly."

Why They Were Deprecated

In version 1.120 — the previous LTS release — all three libraries were marked @deprecated with the message:

"This Web Components consumption model has been discarded. An alternative consumption model is planned for the future."

That alternative has arrived a while ago.

The Better Approach: sap.ui.core.webc.WebComponent

Since version 1.138, SAPUI5 includes a new public base class — sap.ui.core.webc.WebComponent — that lives directly in sap.ui.core. This is not another wrapper library. It's a framework-level integration point.

Here's what's different:

1. Web Components come from npm, not from frozen bundles.

Instead of shipping a static copy of @ui5/webcomponents, the new approach uses ui5-tooling-modules to consume Web Components directly from npm packages at build time. This means your application stays on current Web Component versions — today v2.x — rather than being locked to a frozen v1.18 copy.

2. Integration is at the framework level.

The WebComponent base class in sap.ui.core handles property propagation, aggregation-to-slot mapping, event forwarding, and rendering integration generically. Individual Web Component wrappers are thin — generated metadata, not hand-maintained control code.

3. The framework is already using it internally.

The new integration approach is already used within the framework itself — for example, the Shell and Enterprise Search in the sap.f library consume Web Components through this mechanism. While these specific integrations are currently private/restricted APIs, they validate that the approach works at scale in production scenarios. Public Web Component-based controls built on sap.ui.core.webc.WebComponent will follow.

4. It's public API.

Unlike the sap.ui.webc.* libraries which stayed experimental for their entire lifecycle, sap.ui.core.webc.WebComponent is a public, stable API since 1.138.

What You Should Do

If you never used sap.ui.webc.*: Nothing changes for you. If you want to use Web Components in your SAPUI5 app going forward, read the Using Web Components documentation.

If you used sap.ui.webc.* in a prototype or experiment: Since these libraries were always experimental, now is the time to move to the new approach. The controls you were using (like sap.ui.webc.main.Button or sap.ui.webc.fiori.ShellBar) have no 1:1 migration path because the consumption model changed fundamentally. However, the underlying Web Components are the same — and better, since you now get v2.x instead of v1.18.

If you used sap.ui.webc.* in a productive application despite the experimental status: The libraries will be removed in version 1.147.0. We recommend starting the migration now. The Using Web Components guide is your starting point.

If you're on the 1.120 LTS and planning to upgrade to the next LTS: The previous LTS (1.120) still ships the deprecated sap.ui.webc.* libraries. The next LTS (1.148) will not — they are removed one version earlier in 1.147. If your application depends on these libraries, you must migrate before upgrading to 1.148 LTS.

Timeline

Version Event

1.92 (2021)sap.ui.webc.* introduced as experimental
1.120 (2023)sap.ui.webc.* deprecated — previous LTS
1.138 (2025)sap.ui.core.webc.WebComponent introduced as public API
1.147sap.ui.webc.* libraries removed
1.148Next LTS release — sap.ui.webc.* not available

Summary

The sap.ui.webc.* libraries were an early experiment that taught us a lot about what Web Components integration in SAPUI5 should look like. The answer turned out to be: don't bundle frozen copies — integrate at the framework level and let developers consume Web Components from the ecosystem directly.

That's exactly what the new approach does, and it's available today.

Next step: Using Web Components in SAPUI5

entry.title: Bringing AI Directly Into SAPUI5 Controls - AI UI5 Library
entry.content:
A lot of AI work in the SAP space focuses on agents, assistants, and orchestration. This post is about something smaller in scope: bringing AI directly into SAPUI5 as reusable controls, close to where users interact with data every day.

Users frequently work with text fields, writing descriptions, filling in notes, composing messages. Tasks like improving the tone of a text, fixing spelling mistakes, or translating a field value are small, well-defined, and a good fit for what LLMs do well. This post shares an experiment in packaging that kind of capability into a UI5 control library.

The idea: AI as reusable UI5 controls

The library (be.wl.ai) provides a set of AI-powered UI5 controls that can be dropped into any XML view. A consuming app developer doesn’t need to know anything about AI Core or set up any AI infrastructure, they reference the library, add a control and bind it to a model property:

<TextArea value="{/description}" />

<ai:AIPolishButton text="Polish" value="{/description}" />

The TextArea and the AI button are both bound to the same model property. When the user accepts the AI result, it flows back into the model and the TextArea updates automatically, standard UI5 two-way data binding, nothing custom required.

What’s in the library

The library provides 11 controls covering the most common AI text operations:

https://github.com/lemaiwo/ui5-ai-features

Text transformation (Accept/Reject dialog):

  • AIPolishButton — improves grammar, clarity, and professionalism
  • AIGenerateButton — generates text from a short description
  • AISpellCheckButton — corrects errors and shows a summary of changes
  • AIToneButton — rewrites text in a different tone (formal, casual, persuasive, etc.)
  • AITranslateButton — translates into 12 languages via a dropdown menu

Analysis (read-only results):

  • AISummarizeButton — produces a concise summary
  • AIExtractKeywordsButton — extracts keywords as tokens with copy-to-clipboard
  • AICompareTexts — compares two texts and shows differences in HTML
  • AISentimentIndicator — shows a positive/neutral/negative sentiment badge

Inline assistance:

  • AIInputSuggestion — opens a popover with an AI-suggested improvement
  • AIAutoComplete — completes partial text inline

Every control uses standard UI5 two-way data binding through a value property. Bind it to the same model property as your input field, and the AI result flows back automatically.

Architecture: simple on purpose

The controls connect to a CAP backend service that wraps SAP AI Core’s Orchestration Service. The library creates a single shared OData model internally, app developers never see it.

The key design decision: the controls own their connection to the AI service. The consuming app doesn’t need to configure a model, set up a destination or know anything about AI Core. Import the library, use the controls, done.

Under the hood, all button controls extend a shared AIBaseButton base class that handles the OData call, dialog lifecycle, busy indicators, and accept/reject flow. Building a new AI control takes about 50 lines of code, just override the prompt, icon, and event name.

Design decisions worth noting

A few choices shaped how this library ended up, and they’re worth explaining for anyone considering a similar approach.

The controls own their AI connection. Rather than requiring the consuming app to configure a model or destination, the library manages its own OData connection to the CAP backend internally. This keeps the integration surface small, an app developer just binds a value property, the same way they would with any other UI5 control.

Standard UI5 patterns throughout. The controls use properties, events, and data binding exactly as UI5 developers would expect. There’s no custom state management or framework-specific logic. This makes the controls easier to understand, debug, and extend.

Prompts are exposed as properties. Every control has a prompt property that overrides the default. This makes it straightforward to adapt a control’s behaviour for a specific domain or use case without forking the library.

No SAP-specific beta features. Everything here runs on generally available SAP technology, UI5, CAP, and AI Core Orchestration Service. There’s no dependency on preview APIs or experimental tooling.

Current status

This is a first version and a proof of concept of what’s possible. It works locally with cds watch and the controls are fully functional. The library is structured for deployment to SAP BTP (MTA with HTML5 repo host), but it hasn’t been tested deployed yet.

The code is intentionally kept simple. There’s no streaming, no conversation history, no RAG. Just a text in, text out pattern, which covers a surprising number of real use cases.

Try it yourself

Prerequisites

  • Node.js 20+
  • An SAP AI Core instance (extended plan) with access to the Orchestration Service
  • A deployment of a model like GPT-4o through SAP AI Core

Running locally

The AI controls call SAP AI Core’s Orchestration Service on the backend. To run this locally, you need to use the hybrid approach — your CAP server runs on your machine but binds to a real AI Core service instance on SAP BTP.

  1. Clone the repository:
git clone https://github.com/lemaiwo/ui5-ai-features.git
cd ui5-ai-features 
npm install
  1. Log in to your Cloud Foundry environment:
cf login
  1. Bind to your AI Core instance using the hybrid profile:
npm run bind
  1. Start the dev server with the hybrid profile:
npm run watch-hybrid
  1. Open the app at the URL shown in the terminal:

http://localhost:4004/be.wl.ai.aidemo/index.html

Using the library in your own app

The library lives in app/ai-lib and the demo app in app/aidemo. To use the controls in your own app, add the library as a dependency and start using them in your XML views:

<mvc:View xmlns:ai="be.wl.ai">
    <TextArea value="{/notes}" />
    <ai:AIPolishButton text="Polish" value="{/notes}" />
    <ai:AITranslateButton text="Translate" value="{/notes}" />
    <ai:AISpellCheckButton text="Spell Check" value="{/notes}" />
</mvc:View>

The controls use two-way data binding. Bind both the TextArea and the AI control to the same model property, when the user accepts the AI result, the TextArea updates automatically.

Takeaways

This started as an experiment to see how much AI functionality could be packaged into reusable UI5 controls without requiring app developers to understand the AI layer. The answer is: quite a lot, with a relatively small amount of code.

The text in, text out pattern is simpler than agent-based approaches, but it fits a wide range of real use cases that users encounter daily. It also sits comfortably alongside agent and MCP work, both approaches address different parts of the problem.

The code is available if you want to explore, fork, or adapt it. Feedback and ideas are welcome.

Result

entry.title: Introducing UI5 Plugins for Claude: AI-Powered Development for SAPUI5 and OpenUI5
entry.content:
Introducing UI5 Plugins for Claude: AI-Powered Development for SAPUI5 and OpenUI5

We're introducing UI5 plugins for Claude, an open-source collection of plugins for Claude Code. These plugins are designed to enhance UI5 development workflows with AI assistance.

What Are Claude Code Plugins?

Claude Code is Anthropic's AI-powered CLI for software development. Its plugin system lets domain experts extend Claude's built-in capabilities with specialized knowledge, tools, and skills for a specific technology. With plugins, Claude Code becomes aware of framework-specific conventions, APIs, and best practices. This means developers get more accurate, context-aware assistance without having to explain the fundamentals every time.

Why We Built This

The UI5 framework has a rich but specialized programming model. Developers, especially those new to UI5 or returning after a break, often spend significant time looking up the correct patterns for class definitions, module loading, TypeScript typing, and toolchain configuration. AI assistance can save hours in these situations, but only if the AI has accurate, curated knowledge of the framework.

Rather than relying on the LLM's general training data, which can be outdated or incomplete for framework details, these plugins embed current, maintained guidelines directly into the development workflow. The result is that Claude Code provides UI5-accurate suggestions from Day One, so you don't have to paste documentation into every conversation.

Last year, we published the UI5 MCP server described in Give Your AI Agent Some Tools: Introducing the UI5 MCP Server. The introduction of agent skills over the last year makes some tasks easier to complete compared to using only the UI5 MCP server. MCP servers aren't going away; this just introduces a new way to distribute tasks between MCP servers or skills. The choice depends on the task: Skills work well for tasks described in free text, while MCP servers offer better support for tasks that require precise input and output.

What We Are Releasing

The initial release ships two plugins:

1. UI5 Plugin — Your UI5 Development Companion

The UI5 plugin equips Claude Code with deep knowledge of the SAPUI5 and OpenUI5 ecosystem. It helps you with the following tasks:

  • Create new UI5 projects: Create scaffolded project structures based on best practices without any manual setup.
  • Detect and fix UI5-specific errors: Claude understands UI5 linting rules and applies fixes based on the official guidelines.
  • Access UI5 API documentation: Get accurate, framework-aware answers about controls, modules, events, and APIs instead of generic JavaScript suggestions.

Technically, the plugin is a wrapper around the UI5 MCP server. Install it with a single command:

claude plugin install ui5@claude-plugins-official

Or from within Claude Code:

/plugin install ui5@claude-plugins-official

2. UI5 TypeScript Conversion plugin — Migrate to TypeScript with Confidence

Migrating a JavaScript UI5 project to TypeScript can be tricky. Generic AI tools often handle the required transformations incorrectly. These transformations are necessary for the UI5 class system, the sap.ui.define module loader, runtime-generated getter and setter methods on controls, and library-specific patterns. The UI5 TypeScript Conversion plugin provides Claude Code with a step-by-step conversion playbook.

Install it with:

claude plugin install ui5-typescript-conversion@claude-plugins-official

Get Started

  1. Install Claude Code.
  2. Install one or both plugins as shown above.
  3. Start coding. For example, you can ask Claude Code to create a UI5 project, run UI5 linter, explain an API, or start a TypeScript migration.

The source code for both plugins is available on GitHub at UI5/plugins-claude under the Apache 2.0 license. We welcome contributions, bug reports, and feature requests via GitHub Issues.

You can also join the conversation in the #ai channel of the OpenUI5 Community Slack or ask questions on Stack Overflow using the ui5-plugins-claude tag.

entry.title: Mastering SAPUI5 custom Filter Variants Without a Backend Table
entry.content:
Mastering SAPUI5 Filter Variants Without a Backend Table

Many SAPUI5 developers assume that filter variants require a backend table. You might have even thought: “How can I save user filter preferences without touching a database?”

Here’s the good news: you absolutely can, and it’s cleaner, faster, and fully Fiori-compliant. Using SmartVariantManagement (SVM) and a FilterBar, you can build full variant management entirely client-side, while supporting complex controls like MultiInput, MultiComboBox, and DateRangeSelection.

In this blog, we’ll walk through a real-world implementation with custom development in UI.

1️⃣ Why Backend Tables Are Not Always Needed

Most SAP examples integrate variants with OData services or SmartTables, which gives the impression that server-side storage is required.

But here’s the truth:

  • SmartVariantManagement can store variants for standalone apps.
  • If your app runs in Launchpad, variants are stored in the personalization service.
  • The only requirement is to connect your FilterBar to SVM programmatically.

2️⃣ The View Setup

Here’s a clean XML setup with SVM and a FilterBar:

<smartvariants:SmartVariantManagement
    id="svm"
    showExecuteOnSelection="true"
/>

<fb:FilterBar
    id="filterbarList"
    useToolbar="false"
    search=".onSearch"
    persistencyKey="evaluationFilterBar"
    showFilterConfiguration="true"
    afterVariantLoad=".onAfterVariantLoad"
/>

Key Points:

  • showExecuteOnSelection="true" ensures users can auto-execute searches when a variant is selected.
  • persistencyKey allows local persistence even without backend tables.
  • The FilterBar does not automatically connect to SVM; we handle that in the controller.

3️⃣ Wiring FilterBar to SVM in the Controller

3.1 Register Fetch and Apply Functions

this._oFilterBar = this.byId("filterbarList");
this._oSmartVariantManagement = this.byId("svm");
this._oFilterBar.registerFetchData(this._fetchVariantData.bind(this));
this._oFilterBar.registerApplyData(this._applyVariantData.bind(this));
this._oFilterBar.attachAfterVariantLoad(this._onAfterVariantLoad.bind(this);
  • _fetchVariantData() extracts all filter values into JSON.
  • _applyVariantData() restores values when a variant is selected.
  • afterVariantLoad ensures auto-search or other post-load logic runs.

3.2 Add FilterBar as Personalizable Control

const oPersInfo = new PersonalizableInfo({
    type: "filterBar",
    keyName: "persistencyKey",
    dataSource: "",
    control: this._oFilterBar
});
this._oSmartVariantManagement.addPersonalizableControl(oPersInfo);
  • PersonalizableInfo registers the FilterBar with SVM, enabling full variant management, including Execute on Selection.

3.3 Initialize SmartVariantManagement

this._oSmartVariantManagement.initialise(function () {}, this._oFilterBar);
  • Establishes the connection between SVM and FilterBar.
  • Variants are now fully functional, even without any backend storage.

4️⃣ Handling Complex Filter Controls

Fetch Variant Data : Explicitly fetches all tokens from MultiInput controls so multiple values are captured per filter.

_fetchVariantData() {
    const oData = {};
    this._oFilterBar.getAllFilterItems().forEach(item => {
        const oControl = item.getControl();
        if (!oControl) return;

        if (oControl.isA("sap.m.MultiInput")) {
            oData[item.getName()] = oControl.getTokens().map(t => t.getKey() || t.getText());
        }

        if (oControl.isA("sap.m.DateRangeSelection")) {
            oData[item.getName()] = {
                from: oControl.getDateValue(),
                to: oControl.getSecondDateValue()
            };
        }

        if (oControl.isA("sap.m.MultiComboBox")) {
            oData[item.getName()] = oControl.getSelectedKeys();
        }
    });
    return oData;
}

Apply Variant Data : Ensures all stored tokens are re-applied, supporting multi-value filter selection.

_applyVariantData(oData) {
    if (!oData) return;

    this._oFilterBar.getAllFilterItems().forEach(item => {
        const oControl = item.getControl();
        const vValue = oData[item.getName()];
        if (!oControl) return;

        if (oControl.isA("sap.m.MultiInput")) {
            oControl.removeAllTokens();
            if (Array.isArray(vValue)) {
                vValue.forEach(val => oControl.addToken(new sap.m.Token({ key: val, text: val })));
            }
        }

        if (oControl.isA("sap.m.DateRangeSelection")) {
            oControl.setDateValue(vValue?.from || null);
            oControl.setSecondDateValue(vValue?.to || null);
        }

        if (oControl.isA("sap.m.MultiComboBox")) {
            oControl.setSelectedKeys(vValue || []);
        }
    });

    // Trigger search automatically
    this.onSearch();
}
  • These functions serialize and restore all filter control values, making the variants robust and accurate.

5️⃣ How It Works (Visual Diagram)

Flow Explanation:

  1. FilterBar registers fetch/apply logic for all controls.
  2. PersonalizableInfo connects FilterBar to SVM.
  3. Variants are stored in personalization.
  4. Users can save, load, and auto-execute variants seamlessly.

6️⃣ Key Benefits

  •  Fully functional variant management without a backend table
  • Supports complex controls: MultiInput, MultiComboBox, DateRangeSelection
  • upports complex controls and multi-value filters via tokens
  • Enables Execute on Selection
  • Works in standalone apps or Fiori Launchpad
  • Easy to extend for additional custom controls

7️⃣ Key Takeaways

  1. FilterBar alone is not enough; manual SVM wiring is required.
  2. PersonalizableInfo + addPersonalizableControl() + initialise() connects everything.
  3. Added: Custom fetch/apply functions handle complex controls and **tokenized multi-value filters**.
  4. Custom fetch/apply functions handle complex controls.
  5. Variants can be fully stored client-side, making backend persistence optional.

Bonus: User-Specific Variants

One surprising feature is that these variants respect user profiles automatically. Even though the variants are stored client-side or in FLP personalization, each user sees their own saved variants. This means you can have multiple users using the same app, each with their own filter preferences, without any backend table or custom storage logic.

Key points:

  • Variants are automatically scoped per user in Fiori Launchpad or browser storage.
  • No extra coding is required to separate user preferences.
  • Works seamlessly with Execute on Selection and all complex controls.

With this approach, you give users a robust, professional, Fiori-compliant variant experience—without the overhead of backend tables.

entry.title: More Power, New Name: The UI Flexibility Service Explained
entry.content:
SAPUI5 flexibility for adapting standard SAP apps

SAPUI5 is SAP’s strategic Web framework for SAP Fiori. SAPUI5 flexibility allows developers, key users, and end users to adapt standard apps with simple drag-and-drop tools. Discover all the capabilities here: SAPUI5 Flexibility - All You Need to Know.

 

Key user adaptation

Key user adaptation is built into SAP Fiori Launchpad / SAP Build Work Zone and is included commercially as part of the respective product. Outside these products, the same capabilities are available as a dedicated service, UI5 flexibility for key users, on SAP BTP, enabling key user adaptation for standalone apps, for example, for SAP partners building their own apps. The service is available via BTPEA, BTP Trial, and partner TD&D licenses. See details in the SAP Discovery Center.

 

What's new?

Some time ago, we added the end user personalization capability to the UI5 flexibility for key users service. This allows also business users to easily change the UI of standard apps, helping them work more efficiently. This created confusion, as the service name UI5 flexibility for key users no longer reflects the full range of benefits. We therefore decided to rename the service:

"UI5 flexibility for key users" ➔ "UI flexibility service"

You will see the name being changed now across all SAP assets, e.g.:

 

Apart from the name, nothing changes in the scope or functionality of the UI flexibility service.

entry.title: Deep Entity Handling in SAP UI5 Using OData Service
entry.content:
• Introduction:

A Deep Entity allows you to send and receive hierarchical data (parent + child) in a single request, making your application more efficient, cleaner, and closer to real business scenarios.

In this blog, we will build a complete end-to-end example of a  Deep Entity, covering:

  • Backend implementation using SEGW (ABAP OData)
  • Frontend implementation using SAP UI5
  • Read (Expand) and Create (Deep Insert) operations

Architecture Overview:

SAP UI5 App 

   ↓ 

OData Service (Gateway) 

   ↓ 

Backend Logic (ABAP) 

   ↓ 

Database Tables 

Backend Implementation (ABAP - OData Service):

Step 1: Define Entities in SEGW:

Create: 

  • Header Entity → Sales Order (VBAK) 

  • Item Entity → Sales Order Item (VBAP) 

Step 2: Create Association :

  • Association: Header → Item (1: N)  

  • Navigation Property: znavigation.

Step 3: Create Deep Structure: 

  • Go To: MPC EXT → Source Code-Based. 
  • Create Deep Structure.

 

class ZCL_ZK_DEEP_ENTITY_MPC_EXT definition 
   public 
   inheriting from ZCL_ZK_DEEP_ENTITY_MPC 
   create public . 
public section. 
  types: 
     BEGIN OF ty_deep_str, 
               vbeln              TYPE  vbeln_va, 
               erdat              TYPE  erdat, 
               ernam              TYPE  ernam, 
               auart              TYPE  auart, 
               kunnr              TYPE  kunnr, 
               znavigation TYPE TABLE OF zcl_zk_deep_entity_mpc_ext=>ts_vbap WITH DEFAULT KEY, 
             END OF ty_deep_str . 
protected section. 
   PRIVATE SECTION. 
ENDCLASS. 

CLASS ZCL_ZK_DEEP_ENTITY_MPC_EXT IMPLEMENTATION. 
ENDCLASS.

 

 Step 3: Redefine GET_EXPANDED_ENTITY, GET_EXPANDED_ENTITYSET, CREATE_DEEP_ENTITY. 

Go to: 

DPC_EXT Class → Method: 

Method: /IWBEP/IF_MGW_APPL_SRV_RUNTIME~GET_EXPANDED_ENTITY. 

 

METHOD /iwbep/if_mgw_appl_srv_runtime~get_expanded_entity. 
 
  DATA: ls_deep_str TYPE zcl_zk_deep_entity_mpc_ext=>ty_deep_str, 
         ls_vbak     TYPE zstr_vbak, 
         lt_vbap     TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF zstr_vbap. 
 
  DATA lv_vbeln TYPE vbeln_va. 
 
  "-------------------------------------------------- 
   " Read key 
   "-------------------------------------------------- 
   lv_vbeln = VALUE #( it_key_tab[ name = 'Vbeln' ]-value OPTIONAL ). 
   lv_vbeln = |{ lv_vbeln ALPHA = IN }|. 
 
  "-------------------------------------------------- 
   " Read Header (VBAK) 
   "-------------------------------------------------- 
   SELECT SINGLE 
          vbeln, 
          erdat, 
          ernam, 
          auart, 
          kunnr 
     FROM zdt_vbak 
     INTO _vbak 
     WHERE vbeln = _vbeln. 
 
  IF sy-subrc <> 0. 
     RETURN. 
   ENDIF. 
 
  "-------------------------------------------------- 
   " Read Items (VBAP) 
   "-------------------------------------------------- 
   SELECT 
          vbeln, 
          posnr, 
          matnr, 
          kwmeng, 
          vrkme 
     FROM zdt_vbap 
     INTO TABLE _vbap 
     WHERE vbeln = _vbeln. 
 
  "-------------------------------------------------- 
   " Explicit Header Mapping (IMPORTANT) 
   "-------------------------------------------------- 
   CLEAR ls_deep_str. 
 
  ls_deep_str-vbeln = ls_vbak-vbeln. 
   ls_deep_str-erdat = ls_vbak-erdat. 
   ls_deep_str-ernam = ls_vbak-ernam. 
   ls_deep_str-auart = ls_vbak-auart. 
   ls_deep_str-kunnr = ls_vbak-kunnr. 
 
  "-------------------------------------------------- 
   " Items Mapping 
   "-------------------------------------------------- 
   IF lt_vbap IS NOT INITIAL. 
     ls_deep_str-znavigation = CORRESPONDING #( lt_vbap ). 
   ENDIF. 
 
  "-------------------------------------------------- 
   " Return Deep Entity 
   "-------------------------------------------------- 
   copy_data_to_ref( 
     EXPORTING 
       is_data = ls_deep_str 
     CHANGING 
       cr_data = er_entity 
   ). 
 
  APPEND 'znavigation' TO et_expanded_clauses. 
 
ENDMETHOD.

 

Method: /IWBEP/IF_MGW_APPL_SRV_RUNTIME~GET_EXPANDED_ENTITYSET. 

 

  method /IWBEP/IF_MGW_APPL_SRV_RUNTIME~GET_EXPANDED_ENTITYSET. 
 
      DATA : lt_vbak TYPE ZCL_ZK_DEEP_ENTITY_MPC_EXT=>tt_vbak, 
              lt_vbap TYPE ZCL_ZK_DEEP_ENTITY_MPC_EXT=>tt_vbap, 
              ls_deep type ZCL_ZK_DEEP_ENTITY_MPC_EXT=>ty_deep_str, 
              lt_deep TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ZCL_ZK_DEEP_ENTITY_MPC_EXT=>ty_deep_str. 
 
 
        SELECT FROM zdt_vbak FIELDS VBELN, 
                                     ERDAT, 
                                     ERNAM, 
                                     AUART, 
                                     KUNNR INTO TABLE _vbak. 
 
          LOOP AT lt_vbak ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_vbak>). 

           clear  ls_deep . 
 
            ls_deep = CORRESPONDING #( <fs_vbak> ). 
 
            SELECT FROM zdt_vbap FIELDS VBELN, 
                                         POSNR, 
                                         MATNR, 
                                         KWMENG, 
                                         VRKME 
               WHERE vbeln = @<fs_vbak>-vbeln 
               INTO TABLE _vbap. 
 
              ls_deep-znavigation =  CORRESPONDING #( lt_vbak ). 
 
             APPEND ls_deep to lt_deep. 
 
          ENDLOOP. 

          copy_data_to_ref( 
             EXPORTING 
               is_data = lt_deep 
             CHANGING 
               cr_data = er_entityset 
           ). 

          APPEND 'znavigation' to et_expanded_clauses. 

  endmethod.

 

 Method: /IWBEP/IF_MGW_APPL_SRV_RUNTIME~CREATE_DEEP_ENTITY. 

 

  METHOD /iwbep/if_mgw_appl_srv_runtime~create_deep_entity. 
     DATA : ls_deep    TYPE zcl_zk_deep_entity_mpc_ext=>ty_deep_str, 
            ls_vbak    TYPE zcl_zk_deep_entity_mpc_ext=>ts_vbak, 
            ls_vbap    TYPE zcl_zk_deep_entity_mpc_ext=>ts_vbap, 
 
           ls_vbak2   TYPE zdt_vbak, 
            ls_vbap2   TYPE zdt_vbap, 
 
           lv_vbeln   TYPE zdt_vbak-vbeln, 
            lv_posnr   TYPE zdt_vbap-posnr, 
 
           lv_vbeln_n TYPE zdt_vbak-vbeln, 
            lv_posnr_n TYPE zdt_vbap-posnr. 
 
    io_data_provider->read_entry_data( 
       IMPORTING 
         es_data = ls_deep 
     ). 
 
    SELECT MAX( vbeln ) 
           INTO _vbeln 
           FROM zdt_vbak. 
 
    IF lv_vbeln IS INITIAL. 
       lv_vbeln_n = 1. 
     ELSE. 
       lv_vbeln_n = lv_vbeln + 1. 
     ENDIF. 
 
    lv_vbeln_n = |{ lv_vbeln_n ALPHA = IN }|. 
     ls_vbak-vbeln = lv_vbeln_n. 
     ls_vbak-erdat = sy-datum. 
     ls_vbak-ernam = sy-uname. 
     ls_vbak-auart = ls_deep-auart. 
     ls_vbak-kunnr = ls_deep-kunnr. 
 
    MOVE-CORRESPONDING ls_vbak TO ls_vbak2. 
 
    INSERT zdt_vbak FROM ls_vbak2. 
 
    CLEAR lv_posnr. 
 
    LOOP AT ls_deep-znavigation INTO DATA(ls_deep_item). 
       lv_posnr = lv_posnr + 10. 
 
      ls_vbap-vbeln  = lv_vbeln_n. 
       ls_vbap-posnr  = lv_posnr. 
       ls_vbap-matnr  = ls_deep_item-matnr. 
       ls_vbap-kwmeng = ls_deep_item-kwmeng. 
       ls_vbap-vrkme  = ls_deep_item-vrkme.
